<!--Tsubakimi Lake--> [[Category:Municipal History of Sōsa]] [[Category:Landforms of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Polders]] [[Category:Lagoons of Japan]] [[Category:Municipal History of Asahi (Chiba)]] [[Category:Municipal History of Tōnoshō]] [[Category:Former lakes]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:椿海]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q5120787]] Wikidata name: Tsubakinoumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|qq=}}, near the present-day borders of [[:en:Tōnoshō, Chiba|Tōshō, Chiba]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi, Chiba]], and [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa, Chiba]].<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'s [[:en:Katori District|Katori District, Chiba]], [[:en:Sōsa District, Chiba|Sōsa District, Chiba]], and [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijō District, Chiba]].</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the [[:en:Yamanote Line|Yamanote Line]] loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Tama no Ura]], an ancient bay along [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri Beach]], was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] [[:en:Dugout canoe|Dugout canoe]] discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|qq=}}, were built overlooking the lake during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|qq=}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q5120787]] Wikidata name: Tsubakinoumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|qq=|1=Kujūkuri Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5120855}}, near the present-day borders of [[:en:Tōnoshō, Chiba|Tōshō, Chiba]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi, Chiba]], and [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa, Chiba]].<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'s [[:en:Katori District|Katori District, Chiba]], [[:en:Sōsa District, Chiba|Sōsa District, Chiba]], and [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijō District, Chiba]].</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the [[:en:Yamanote Line|Yamanote Line]] loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Tama no Ura]], an ancient bay along [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri Beach]], was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] [[:en:Dugout canoe|Dugout canoe]] discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|qq=|1=Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}}, were built overlooking the lake during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|qq=|1=Kaijō Lagoon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595047}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|qq=|1=Kamakazu ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|qq=|1=Kamakazu ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

[[sn:Tsubakimi Lake]] [[sn:E6%A4%BF%E6%B5%B7]]
<!--Tsubakimi Lake--> [[Category:Municipal History of Sōsa]] [[Category:Landforms of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Polders]] [[Category:Lagoons of Japan]] [[Category:Municipal History of Asahi (Chiba)]] [[Category:Municipal History of Tōnoshō]] [[Category:Former lakes]] [[en:Tsubakimi Lake]]<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:椿海]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q5120787]] Wikidata name: Tsubakinoumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|qq=|1=Kujūkuri Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5120855}}, near the present-day borders of [[:en:Tōnoshō, Chiba|Tōshō, Chiba]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi, Chiba]], and [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa, Chiba]].<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'s [[:en:Katori District|Katori District, Chiba]], [[:en:Sōsa District, Chiba|Sōsa District, Chiba]], and [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijō District, Chiba]].</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the [[:en:Yamanote Line|Yamanote Line]] loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Tama no Ura]], an ancient bay along [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri Beach]], was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] [[:en:Dugout canoe|Dugout canoe]] discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|qq=|1=Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}}, were built overlooking the lake during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|qq=|1=Kaijō Lagoon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595047}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|qq=|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椿海]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q5120787]] Wikidata name: Tsubakinoumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|1=Kujūkuri Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5120855}}, near the present-day borders of [[:en:Tōnoshō, Chiba|Tōshō, Chiba]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi, Chiba]], and [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa, Chiba]].<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'s [[:en:Katori District|Katori District, Chiba]], [[:en:Sōsa District, Chiba|Sōsa District, Chiba]], and [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijō District, Chiba]].</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the [[:en:Yamanote Line|Yamanote Line]] loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Tama no Ura]], an ancient bay along [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri Beach]], was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] [[:en:Dugout canoe|Dugout canoe]] discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|1=Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}}, were built overlooking the lake during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|1=Kaijō Lagoon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595047}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椿海]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q5120787]] Wikidata name: Tsubakinoumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|1=Kujūkuri Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5120855}}, near the present-day borders of [[:en:Tōnoshō, Chiba|Tōshō, Chiba]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi, Chiba]], and [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa, Chiba]].<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'s [[:en:Katori District|Katori District, Chiba]], [[:en:Sōsa District, Chiba|Sōsa District, Chiba]], and [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijō District, Chiba]].</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the [[:en:Yamanote Line|Yamanote Line]] loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Tama no Ura]], an ancient bay along [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri Beach]], was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] [[:en:Dugout canoe|Dugout canoe]] discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|1=Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}}, were built overlooking the lake during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|1=Kaijō Lagoon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595047}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椿海]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q5120787]] Wikidata name: Tsubakinoumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|1=Kujūkuri Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5120855}}, near the present-day borders of [[:en:Tōnoshō, Chiba|Tōshō, Chiba]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi, Chiba]], and [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa, Chiba]].<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'s [[:en:Katori District|Katori District, Chiba]], [[:en:Sōsa District, Chiba|Sōsa District, Chiba]], and [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijō District, Chiba]].</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the [[:en:Yamanote Line|Yamanote Line]] loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Tama no Ura]], an ancient bay along [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri Beach]], was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] [[:en:Dugout canoe|Dugout canoe]] discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|1=Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}}, were built overlooking the lake during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|1=Kaijō Lagoon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595047}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椿海]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q5120787]] Wikidata name: Tsubakinoumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|1=Kujūkuri Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5120855}}, near the present-day borders of [[:en:Tōnoshō, Chiba|Tōshō, Chiba]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi, Chiba]], and [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa, Chiba]].<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'s [[:en:Katori District|Katori District, Chiba]], [[:en:Sōsa District, Chiba|Sōsa District, Chiba]], and [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijō District, Chiba]].</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the [[:en:Yamanote Line|Yamanote Line]] loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Tama no Ura]], an ancient bay along [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri Beach]], was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] [[:en:Dugout canoe|Dugout canoe]] discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|1=Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}}, were built overlooking the lake during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|1=Kaijō Lagoon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595047}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椿海]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q5120787]] Wikidata name: Tsubakinoumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|1=Kujūkuri Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5120855}}, near the present-day borders of [[:en:Tōnoshō, Chiba|Tōshō, Chiba]], [[:en:Asahi, Chiba|Asahi, Chiba]], and [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa, Chiba]].<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]'s [[:en:Katori District|Katori District, Chiba]], [[:en:Sōsa District, Chiba|Sōsa District, Chiba]], and [[:en:Kaijō District, Chiba|Kaijō District, Chiba]].</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the [[:en:Yamanote Line|Yamanote Line]] loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Tama no Ura]], an ancient bay along [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri Beach]], was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]] / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]] [[:en:Dugout canoe|Dugout canoe]] discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|1=Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}}, were built overlooking the lake during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|1=Kaijō Lagoon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595047}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the [[:en:Kurobe River (Chiba)|Kurobe River (Chiba)]] into [[:en:Katori Sea|Lake Katori]], avoiding the hazardous waters off [[:en:Cape Inubō|Inubōsaki]]<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] **18 (1590)**, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] entered [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]]. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}
[[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|椿海|version=98146921|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Geography of Chiba Prefecture]]
}}

At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by [[:en:Keichō|Keichō (era)]] 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero y Aberrucia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō era]], the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * [[:en:List of lakes of Japan|List of lakes and marshes in Japan]] * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.sosa.lg.jp/sp/page/page001250.html Sōsa City - Legends and Old Stories - Tsubaki Sea] * {{Cite web|和書|title=椿海について書かれた資料を知りたい。 |url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/detail?page=ref_view&id=1000234657 |website=Reference Cooperative Database |publisher=National Diet Library}}
