revision.text:
[[File:坂東地方の牧場と街道.jpg|thumb|Map of the Bandō region. Tsubaki Lake is shown near the right edge of Shimōsa Province (light green).]] '''Tsubaki Sea''' (椿海, Tsubaki no Umi, Tsubaki Umi) or '''Tsubaki Lake'''{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}} was a lake that existed until the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} in the northern part of the {{ill|Kujūkuri Plain|ja|九十九里平野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūkuri Plain|1=Kujukuri Plain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5120855}}, near the present-day borders of {{ill|Tōnoshō, Chiba|en|Tōnoshō, Chiba|WD=Q1342834|lt=Tōshō, Chiba|1=Tōnoshō, Chiba}}, {{ill|Asahi, Chiba|en|Asahi, Chiba|WD=Q637868|lt=Asahi, Chiba|1=Asahi, Chiba}}, and {{ill|Sōsa|en|Sōsa|WD=Q858367|lt=Sōsa, Chiba|1=Sōsa}}.<ref group="Note">It was located at the border of {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}'s {{ill|Katori District|en|Katori District|WD=Q780284|lt=Katori District, Chiba|1=Katori District}}, {{ill|Sōsa District, Chiba|en|Sōsa District, Chiba|WD=Q7667621|lt=Sōsa District, Chiba|1=Sōsa District, Chiba}}, and {{ill|Kaijō District, Chiba|en|Kaijō District, Chiba|WD=Q6347746|lt=Kaijō District, Chiba|1=Kaijō District, Chiba}}.</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite Kotobank |word=椿海 |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |accessdate=2022-07-01}}</ref> In maps from the Edo period, such as the ''Shimōsa no Kuni-zu'' (held at Funabashi City Nishi Library), it is labeled as '''Ōta no Kotsu'''.
According to tradition, it measured three ''ri'' (12 [[:en:Kilometers|Kilometers]]) from east to west and one and a half ''ri'' (6 kilometers) from north to south, covering approximately 51 [[:en:square kilometers|square kilometers]], or about 7,200 [[:en:Hectares|Hectares]], an area large enough to fit inside the {{ill|Yamanote Line|en|Yamanote Line|WD=Q693036|lt=Yamanote Line|1=Yamanote Line}} loop{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|和書|title=椿海の干拓 |url=https://suido-ishizue.jp/kokuei/kanto/Prefectures/1201/1201.html#t2 |website=大利根用水農業水利事業 -水土の礎- |accessdate=2022-09-12 |publisher=一般社団法人農業農村整備情報総合センター}}</ref>.
== Overview == The ''Katori-shi'' describes a legend: "According to ancient elders, a massive camellia tree once stood here. When it bloomed, the sky turned red, and when its petals fell, the ground became a crimson carpet. When the great tree reached the end of its life and collapsed, the remaining roots became a lake, which was named Tsubaki Sea. The upper branches were called Kazusa, and the lower branches were called Shimōsa." However, in reality, it is believed that an inlet of {{ill|Kujūkuri Beach|en|Kujūkuri Beach|WD=Q996212|lt=Tama no Ura|1=Kujūkuri Beach}}, an ancient bay along {{ill|Kujūkuri Beach|en|Kujūkuri Beach|WD=Q996212|lt=Kujūkuri Beach|1=Kujūkuri Beach}}, was blocked by sand carried by coastal currents, forming a lagoon{{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|1984|p=566}}.
The {{ill|Kuriyama River|en|Kuriyama River|WD=Q6446101|lt=Kuriyama River|1=Kuriyama River}} / Tsubaki Sea water system has yielded approximately 40% of all known {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period|1=Jōmon period}} {{ill|Dugout canoe|en|Dugout canoe|WD=Q596073|lt=Dugout canoe|1=Dugout canoe}} discoveries in Japan<ref>''Ancient Japan's Military Navigation History, Volume 1'', p. 43.</ref>, and ancient Kofuns, such as the {{ill|Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|ja|御前鬼塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozen Oni-zuka Kofun|1=Q11488824|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488824}}, were built overlooking the lake during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}<ref>''Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten'', p. 192.</ref>. The area was later reclaimed and became known as "Higata Hachiman-goku" (Eighty Thousand Koku of Reclaimed Land), but historical records suggest that in ancient times, a harbor called {{ill|Kaijō Lagoon|ja|海上潟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaijō Lagoon|1=Q109595047|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595047}} (Kaijō-tsu) near the northeastern coastline of the Kujūkuri Plain was frequently used as a port. This is evident from poetry in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}, such as:
*"Natsu asa hiku / kaijōgata no / okitsu shima ni / tori wa sudakedo / kimi wa oto mo sezu" (Summer morning flax is pulled at Kaijō Lagoon, birds call from the distant sandbanks, but my lord makes no sound) (7-1176).
*"Kashima no saki ni... yūshio no / michi no todomi ni / mifunako o / hikitate te / yobitate te / mifune de naba / hama mo se ni... kaijō no / sono tsu o sashite / kimi ga kogi yuka ba" (At the tip of Kashima... the evening tide comes in, as the sailors raise their voices, calling to set sail; heading for that port of Kaijō) (9-1780)<ref>''Hirano wa Kataru (Nihon o Shiru)'', p. 150,161.</ref>.
These references indicate that in ancient times, this area was a key stop along the water route leading from the {{ill|Kurobe River (Chiba)|en|Kurobe River (Chiba)|WD=Q6446264|lt=Kurobe River (Chiba)|1=Kurobe River (Chiba)}} into {{ill|Katori Sea|en|Katori Sea|WD=Q11667182|lt=Lake Katori|1=Katori Sea}}, avoiding the hazardous waters off {{ill|Cape Inubō|en|Cape Inubō|WD=Q2937063|lt=Inubōsaki|1=Cape Inubō}}<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Aoyama Hiroo |title=干拓以前の潟湖とその機能 : 椿海と下総の水上交通試論 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00001288 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |year=2004 |volume=118 |pages=193-217 |doi=10.15024/00001288 |naid=120005748477 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館}}</ref>.
== Development in the Edo Period == === Background of Reclamation === On the **first day of the eighth month of** {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} **18 (1590)**, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} entered {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}. At that time, the city of Edo had only about 100 thatched-roof houses. However, by {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō (era)|1=Keichō}} 14 (1609), according to the account of {{ill|Don Rodrigo|ja|ドン・ロドリゴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Don Rodrigo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ロドリゴ・デ・ビベロ|1=Rodrigo de Vivero, 1st Count of Valle de Orizaba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2624405}}, the population had already grown to 150,000. By the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}}, the samurai population reached 500,000–600,000, with another 500,000 merchants and laborers{{Sfn|Tokyo Bay History|p=112-113}}. To meet the growing food demand in Edo, the **Tonegawa Eastward Diversion Project** was carried out, and maritime trade routes were opened for transporting rice from **Dewa Province**. The reclamation of Tsubaki Sea was part of this effort{{refnest|group="Note"|**Kawamura Zuiken**, who established the **East and West Shipping Routes**, was also involved in the reclamation of Tsubaki Sea<ref>[https://www.city.sakata.lg.jp/smph/sangyo/kanko/zuiken_sakata.html 河村瑞賢～西廻り航路を開拓したプロジェクトリーダー] - Sakata City. Retrieved August 24, 2022.</ref>.}}.
=== Reclamation of Tsubaki Sea === The first known reclamation attempt was proposed during the **Genna era (1615–1624)** by **Sugiyama Saburōemon**, a merchant from Edo{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}, but the **Tokugawa shogunate** denied permission{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}. Later, during the **Kanbun era (1661–1673)**, **Shirai Jirōemon** petitioned for reclamation, leading to an on-site inspection by **Ina Tadatsune**, but opposition from local villages relying on the lake for irrigation led to rejection once again{{sfn|Heibonsha|1996|pp=44-45}}{{sfn|Chiba Prefectural Historical Research Foundation|2007|pp=817-820}}.
Further efforts, challenges, and eventual success in reclaiming Tsubaki Sea continued throughout the late **17th century**, eventually forming highly fertile farmland known as **Higata Hachiman-goku**. == See Also == * {{ill|List of lakes of Japan|en|List of lakes of Japan|WD=Q1540607|lt=List of lakes and marshes in Japan|1=List of lakes of Japan}} * {{ill|Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|ja|鎌数伊勢大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamatsu Ise Grand Shrine|1=Kamakazu Ise-daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651009}}
